own_review.line: reloadFunctions loop (new code, next.put on the create-new branch)
own_review.issue: Duplicate-NAR classloader leak: if two NARs in the directory declare the same function name, the first one processed is placed into `next` (possibly a reused, open previous FunctionArchive); the second does remaining.remove(name)==null, so prev==null, and next.put(name, new FunctionArchive(...)) silently displaces the first entry. The displaced instance ends up in neither `next` nor `functionsToClose`, so its NarClassLoader/extracted NAR is never closed. Fix: capture the return of next.put and add a non-null displaced value to toClose. (Same flaw exists in pre-existing ConnectorUtils.reloadConnectors, but copying it here is still a defect of this PR.)

MD5 helper duplicated verbatim from ConnectorUtils.computeArchiveMd5Hex/calculateMd5Hex (ConnectorUtils.java:61-66); the whole reloadFunctions body is also a near-verbatim copy of reloadConnectors. Common methods should be extracted to a shared utility in pulsar-functions-utils instead of duplicating. This is the human reviewer's point.

Behavioral change to the existing 4-arg constructor: it now eagerly reads the whole archive to compute MD5 and wraps IOException in UncheckedIOException. In searchForFunctions this unchecked exception is swallowed by the pre-existing catch(Throwable), so an IO error during hashing now silently drops a function that previously (no IO in constructor) would have been registered - fail-open behavior with only a warn log. Throwing UncheckedIOException from a constructor is also a questionable API.

Unsynchronized read-modify-write race: volatile only gives visibility. reloadBuiltinFunctions (FunctionsImpl.java:764) can be invoked concurrently by admins; two concurrent reloads snapshot the same this.functions, both may reuse the same prev instances, and one's functionsToClose can contain archives the other kept in its winning map - leaving closed archives in the active map (IllegalStateException on getFunctionPackage) or leaking the loser's new archives. The old full-replace code never put old instances into the new map, so this race is newly harmful; reloadFunctions should be synchronized.

A transient IO error reading an unchanged NAR (getFunctionDefinition or computeArchiveMd5Hex throws before remaining.remove) leaves the active archive in `remaining`, so it is closed and the function vanishes from the active set until a later successful reload. Roughly parity with old full-reload behavior, but defeats the PR's reuse goal on transient errors; also drops the previous 'Found function' info log.

points.point: Missing test case for a NAR newly added between scans (tests cover unchanged/changed/removed/missing-dir only)
points.note: Verified: none of the four tests covers the brand-new-function path; real, actionable coverage gap in new code.

points.point: computeArchiveMd5Hex/calculateMd5Hex indirection; same two-level indirection in ConnectorUtils (lines 63-67); cross-cutting cleanup
points.note: Substantially covers the human's sole point: identifies the duplicated MD5 helper region and the ConnectorUtils parallel, suggests cleanup. Line citations accurate.

points.point: Constructor wraps IOException in UncheckedIOException; searchForFunctions catches Throwable and silently skips the function; callers never see the IOException
points.note: Verified real fail-open behavior introduced by eager MD5 in the constructor; matches my finding.

points.point: Swap-then-close ordering means old and new archives coexist briefly; consistent with ConnectorsManager
points.note: Accurate (ConnectorsManager.java:104-105) but explicitly framed as a non-concern with no action.

missed: Duplicate-NAR same-name displacement leak in FunctionUtils.reloadFunctions (displaced archive neither retained nor added to functionsToClose)
Unsynchronized reload read-modify-write race (concurrent reloadBuiltinFunctions can leave closed archives in the active map or leak)

points.point: Each archive's content is hashed twice per reload cycle; searchForFunctions should use the 5-arg constructor with precomputed MD5
points.note: Fabricated inefficiency: reloadFunctions computes the MD5 once and passes it to the 5-arg constructor (no recompute), and searchForFunctions is not invoked during reload - each NAR is hashed exactly once per cycle. The suggested fix would change nothing.

points.point: volatile gives visibility but reloadFunctions is not atomic; readers holding evicted archives get IllegalStateException; acceptable, same as old code
points.note: Facts verified (ThreadRuntime.java ~126 does call getFunction; readers had the same exposure before), but it explicitly dismisses concurrency concerns and never identifies the real reload-vs-reload read-modify-write race.

points.point: Identical writeMinimalNar/sampleDefinition helpers duplicated across the two new test files; extract shared test utility
points.note: Verified: both test files contain verbatim-identical helpers; real, actionable cleanup.

points.point: UncheckedIOException in constructor is intentional, consistent with Connector.java; searchForFunctions catches Throwable so failures are handled gracefully; no change needed
points.note: Facts accurate (Connector.java:50-62 does the same), but framed as pure reassurance with no action; misses that this is a new silent-drop failure mode for previously-registerable functions.

points.point: computeArchiveMd5Hex is public but only used in-package; visibility could be tightened in a follow-up
points.note: Accurate but a non-blocking visibility nit, self-deferred to follow-up.

missed: Duplicate-NAR same-name displacement leak in FunctionUtils.reloadFunctions
Production code duplication of the MD5 helper (and reload routine) verbatim from ConnectorUtils - the human's sole point (v7 only flagged test-helper duplication)
Unsynchronized reload read-modify-write race on concurrent reloadBuiltinFunctions
exceeds: Test helper duplication across the two new test files

qualitative: Both candidates missed the two substantive defects: the duplicate-NAR displaced-archive leak in FunctionUtils.reloadFunctions and the unsynchronized reload read-modify-write race that the volatile keyword only papers over. v5 is the stronger review: three verified, actionable points including the UncheckedIOException fail-open and the missing added-NAR test, and its duplication point substantially lands on the human's sole comment about extracting the shared MD5 helper. v7 reads fluently but its lead point fabricates a double-hashing inefficiency that does not exist (the MD5 is computed once and passed as precomputed), and its remaining points are mostly reassurance-framed observations ('no change needed', 'acceptable', 'follow-up') with only the test-helper duplication being a real actionable catch; it also missed the human's production-code duplication point. Neither candidate fabricated safe behavior at the leak site this round (no false 'goes into toClose' claims), so absence-fabrications are zero for both; v7's error is an invented problem rather than an invented safety guarantee.
